message.content:
I'm planning a rename of all APCS0 quoins (the `apcs_*` identifiers in `Tools/apck/APCS0-SpecTop.adoc`) into a new shape `apcsXY_word`. I need to know every file OUTSIDE APCS0 that references APCS0's quoin names, so the rename can cascade cleanly.

**Search scope**: Tools/apck/ and any .md or .adoc file in the repo that might reference APCS0 quoins.

**Patterns to find** (AsciiDoc/Markdown quoin references):
- `{apcs_...}` — linked-term references
- `:apcs_...:` — attribute declarations
- `[[apcs_...]]` — anchor definitions
- `<<apcs_...,` — cross-reference links
- Prose references like "see `apcs_regex_scan`" or bare `apcs_xxx` in code blocks

**Exclude**: `Tools/apck/APCS0-SpecTop.adoc` itself (that file IS being renamed, don't list its internal references).

**Deliverable**: a compact list grouped by file. For each file that references APCS0 quoins, report:
- File path
- Distinct `apcs_*` quoin names referenced
- Total reference count

If you find zero external references, say so clearly — that simplifies the migration scope.

Quick thoroughness is fine — this is an enumeration, not deep analysis. Under 300 words.

content.text: I'll systematically search for all external references to APCS0 quoins outside the SpecTop file. Let me start by understanding the quoin naming pattern and then search across the repo.

content.content:

115	
116	
117	== Overview
118	
119	This document defines the formal vocabulary for the detection pipeline in Ann's PHI Clipbuddy.
120	It specifies the entities the algorithm operates on, the resources it consults, the procedures it performs, and the execution sites at which each discerner runs.
121	
122	The application specification (APCAS) owns the user experience.
123	The prototype specification (APCPS) owns implementation details, tech stack, data sources, wire-format JSON schemas, and the container image contents.
124	This document owns the detection pipeline's conceptual structure — the terms that must be precise for the pipeline to be reasoned about correctly.
125	
126	The pipeline has three named procedures with ten parallel discerners between the first and second.
127	{apcs_normalization}
128	produces the canonical input.
129	Ten discerners each run as a pure function of
130	{apcs_normalized_text},
131	producing their own intimately-named finding type;
132	seven of them run inside the Rust application and three of them run inside
133	{apcs_container}.
134	{apcs_combining}
135	fuses the ten finding streams into
136	{apcs_unified_finding_s}
137	covering regions of
138	{apcs_normalized_text}.
139	{apcs_anonymization}
140	produces
141	{apcs_anonymized_text}.
142	
143	Byte offsets, character arithmetic, and tokenizer mechanics live in code.
144	This vocabulary talks about regions of
145	{apcs_normalized_text} —
146	the addressable units of detection — and leaves their concrete representation as an implementation concern.
147	
148	
149	== Input
150	
151	[[apcs_clipboard_content]]
152	{apcs_clipboard_content}::
153	Raw content read from the system clipboard on window focus.
154	May be HTML or plain text.
155	Consumed once, then the system clipboard is cleared.
156	
157	[[apcs_epic_html]]
158	{apcs_epic_html}::
159	HTML produced by Epic's "Copy All" function.
160	Semi-structured: bold labels for demographics, section headers for clinical categories, narrative freetext for clinical prose, signature blocks for provider attribution.
161	The structural formatting is the primary advantage over plain text — labels anchor PHI detection with high precision.
162	
163	[[apcs_clinical_gate]]
164	{apcs_clinical_gate}::
165	A heuristic that decides whether
166	{apcs_clipboard_content}
167	is clinical.
168	Scans for known label patterns (Patient:, DOB:, MRN:, etc.).
169	Acceptance threshold: two or more recognized labels present.
170	Content that fails the
171	{apcs_clinical_gate}
172	is not processed; the triage view clears.
173	
174	
175	== Parsed Structure
176	
177	The parsed structure captures the semantic regions Epic's HTML encodes — labeled demographic fields, section headers, narrative prose.
178	Parsing is an implementation-level step of
179	{apcs_normalization};
180	this vocabulary exists because the narrative discussion of individual discerners refers to structural categories when explaining how they decide.
181	Region membership belongs to the concept model; byte offsets and string arithmetic do not.
182	
183	[[apcs_document]]
184	{apcs_document}::
185	The result of parsing
186	{apcs_epic_html}.
187	A sequence of typed
188	{apcs_span_s}
189	whose concatenated text contributions form
190	{apcs_normalized_text}.
191	
192	[[apcs_span]]
193	{apcs_span}::
194	A typed region within the
195	{apcs_document}.
196	Each
197	{apcs_span}
198	has a kind, an optional label, and text content.
199	Three kinds:
200	{apcs_labeled_field},

content.content: 153:  apcs_phi_category values would eliminate the conflation.

91:Ten discerners across two placements, each a pure function of `apcs_normalized_text`, feeding a single `apcs_evidence` pool; `apcs_combining` (deferred) unifies. See APCS0 for the conceptual vocabulary and APCPS for concrete parameterization and wire format.
94:- `apcs_regex_scan` — structural patterns (SSN, phone, email, dates, addresses, zip, labeled identifiers)
95:- `apcs_label_scan` — words following Epic labels (Patient:, Attending:, Facility:, etc.)
96:- `apcs_surname_scan` — US Census surname dictionary
97:- `apcs_firstname_scan` — SSA first-name dictionary
98:- `apcs_city_scan` — US cities dictionary
99:- `apcs_english_scan` — common-English-word whitelist (suppression evidence)
100:- `apcs_medical_scan` — medical-term whitelist (suppression evidence)
103:- `apcs_stanford_scan` — `StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base` (HuggingFace transformers, PyTorch CPU)
104:- `apcs_spacy_scan` — scispaCy `en_core_sci_md` (POS, dependency parse, biomedical NER)
105:- `apcs_stanza_scan` — Stanza English UD pipeline (POS, dependency parse, OntoNotes NER)

105:| Container findings | `apcs_container` on completion | `{N}.json` — consolidated output of all three container discerners (published atomically via `{N}.json.tmp` → rename) |
122:[Omitted long matching line]
173:Ten discerners, seven running inside the Rust application and three inside the container, each a pure function of `apcs_normalized_text`. Each emits an intimately-typed finding into the evidence pool. Combining rules are deferred; the prototype currently uses a stand-in that treats Rust-side findings only, and the anonymized output `{N}-out.txt` reflects that stand-in. Full combining is a later pace once empirical evidence from real clinical text has flowed through all ten discerners.
176:                                 apcs_clipboard_content
180:                                   apcs_normalization  (Rust)
182:                                 apcs_normalized_text  (= {N}-in.txt)
191:                     apcs_evidence
193:                     apcs_combining  (DEFERRED)
195:                 apcs_unified_finding_s
197:                    apcs_anonymization  (Rust)
199:                    apcs_anonymized_text  (= {N}-out.txt)
239:Five separate scans, each parameterized by one dictionary, each emitting its own intimate finding type. All case-insensitive; all use aho-corasick automata built from lowercase entries; all scan the lowercased `apcs_normalized_text` while preserving original case for display. All unconditional — every match emits a finding regardless of whitelist membership, and combining is responsible for interpreting overlaps. All run in the Rust application.
243:| `apcs_surname_scan` | surnames.txt | NAME | PHI candidate — emit on match |
244:| `apcs_firstname_scan` | firstnames.txt | NAME | PHI candidate — emit on match |
245:| `apcs_city_scan` | cities.txt | ADDRESS | PHI candidate (may remap to FACILITY under combining) — emit on match |
246:| `apcs_english_scan` | english_whitelist.txt | (none) | Suppression evidence — emit on match |
247:| `apcs_medical_scan` | medical_whitelist.txt | (none) | Suppression evidence — emit on match |
253:All three run inside `apcs_container` (see Container Architecture below). Each consumes `{N}-in.txt` (the normalized text) and contributes a key to the consolidated `{N}.json`.
257:| `apcs_stanford_scan` | `StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base` (HuggingFace transformers, PyTorch CPU) | container | PHI named entities with native 8-label taxonomy |
258:| `apcs_spacy_scan` | scispaCy `en_core_sci_md` | container | POS tags, morphological features, dependency parse, biomedical NER |
259:| `apcs_stanza_scan` | Stanza English UD pipeline (default English package) | container | POS (UD + language-specific), morphological features, lemma, dependency parse, OntoNotes-style NER |
267:Runs of identical labels form a single entity. This taxonomy is read from `config.json:id2label` at container image build time — no hardcoded mapping on the Rust side. Mapping these native labels to `apcs_phi_category` is a combining concern (deferred).
317:1. Clipbuddy writes `{N}-in.txt` containing `apcs_normalized_text` to `$HOME/apcjd/`.
358:- `start`/`end` are character offsets into `apcs_normalized_text`.

content.text: Let me get more detailed counts for APCPS, which has many references:

content.content:
1	# Ann's PHI Clipbuddy — Prototype Specification
2	
3	## Scope
4	
5	This document specifies the engineering pipeline for the proof-of-life prototype. It covers detection algorithms, tech stack, data sources, wire-format JSON schemas, the container image, project structure, and scope boundaries. It does NOT cover the user experience — see [APCAS-Specification.md](APCAS-Specification.md) for the UX plan — nor the detection pipeline's conceptual vocabulary, which lives in [APCS0-SpecTop.adoc](APCS0-SpecTop.adoc).
6	
7	This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec.
8	
9	## Scope Boundaries
10	
11	The prototype deliberately excludes:
12	
13	- **No JavaScript** — All rendering logic is Rust. The Tauri webview is a passive display surface receiving complete HTML from the Rust backend. Inline `onclick` attributes use the Tauri bridge primitive (`window.__TAURI__.core.invoke`) for command dispatch only — no `.js` files, no JS application logic, no JS state management. This eliminates the language-boundary bug class (e.g., byte-offset vs char-offset mismatch at the Rust-JS serialization boundary).
14	- **No persistence** — no audit trail, no history of anonymized notes beyond the journal directory artifacts
15	- **One patient at a time** — no batch processing in the interactive path; batch assay (`apcab`) is dev-only
16	- **No configuration** — no custom blacklists, no user preferences, no settings UI
17	- **No click-to-highlight** — clicking a finding does not scroll/highlight in the document preview (future feature)
18	- **No multi-word merge in Rust-side scans** — adjacent name hits from the dictionary scans are individually flagged, not merged; container-side NER discerners (Stanford, Stanza, spaCy) may produce multi-token spans natively
19	
20	## Tech Stack
21	
22	### Rust Application
23	
24	| Layer | Crate | Version | License | Rationale |
25	|-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------|
26	| Clipboard | `arboard` | 3.x | MIT/Apache | Only crate with cross-platform HTML clipboard read (`get_html()`) |
27	| HTML parsing | `scraper` | latest | MIT | DOM + CSS selectors, built on Mozilla's html5ever |
28	| Dictionary matching | `aho-corasick` | latest | MIT/Unlicense | Single-pass multi-pattern O(n) regardless of dictionary size |
29	| Pattern matching | `regex` | latest | MIT/Apache | Structural PHI patterns |
30	| Word boundaries | `unicode-segmentation` | latest | MIT/Apache | UAX#29 compliant word boundary detection |
31	| GUI shell | `tauri` | 2.x | MIT | Rust-rendered HTML/CSS frontend, system webview as passive display |
32	| File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows |
33	| NSPasteboard FFI (macOS) | `objc2` + `objc2-foundation` + `objc2-app-kit` | 0.6.4 / 0.3.2 / 0.3.2 | MIT | Declared-UTI enumeration for multi-flavor clipboard harvest. Versions pinned to match whatever Tauri already pulls transitively (single objc2 tree resolution). Gated `[target.'cfg(target_os = "macos")'.dependencies]`. |
34	| JSON deserialization | `serde` + `serde_json` | 1.x | MIT/Apache | Read container's consolidated findings file |
35	
36	All Rust crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows — both ship with the OS). The webview receives pre-rendered HTML from Rust on every state change; no JavaScript application code exists in the project.
37	
38	ONNX Runtime (`ort` crate), previously used by the Stanford spike binary `apcnsa`, is no longer used by the production pipeline — all neural inference now runs inside the container. The `apcnsa` spike binary retains the `ort` dependency for its own offline assay runs; see "Historical spike" note under Container Discerners.
39	
40	### Container Image
41	
42	| Layer | Package | Rationale |
43	|-------|---------|-----------|
44	| Base image | `python:3.11-slim` (or pinned equivalent) | Minimal Python 3.11 footprint |
45	| Neural inference | `transformers`, `torch` (CPU-only wheel) | Native PyTorch runtime for `StanfordAIMI/stanford-deidentifier-base` |
46	| Tokenization | `tokenizers` | HuggingFace fast tokenizer (bundled transitively) |
47	| Biomedical NLP | `scispacy`, `spacy`, model `en_core_sci_md` | Clinical-domain spaCy pipeline — POS, dependency parse, biomedical NER |
48	| General NLP | `stanza` (English UD package) | POS, morphological features, dependency parse, OntoNotes-style NER as a second independent NER signal |
49	
50	`scispacy` is layered on `spacy` and tracks upstream spaCy releases with occasional lag; the Dockerfile pins both to a compatible pair. Torch is installed as the CPU-only wheel explicitly — GPU wheels are unusable inside a Linux container on macOS (Metal/MPS does not pass through) and would only bloat the image.

content.text: Perfect. Now let me count the total references and check if there are any other .adoc or .md files I might have missed:
Let me check APCAS-Specification more carefully:
Let me check the actual content to see if APCAS has any apcs_ references:

content.content:
1	# Ann's PHI Clipbuddy — Product Specification
2	
3	## Motivation
4	
5	Clinicians using Epic EHR frequently need to consult external AI tools — particularly Open Evidence, a medical AI assistant for diagnostic evaluation and literature synthesis. Open Evidence is a web chat interface where clinicians paste clinical questions enriched with patient context: lab values, history, symptoms, medications.
6	
7	The problem: this patient context is Protected Health Information (PHI) under HIPAA. Open Evidence explicitly advises users not to enter PHI. The HIPAA Safe Harbor method defines 18 categories of identifiers that must be removed for de-identification. Today, clinicians either skip the AI consultation entirely (suboptimal care) or manually scrub text before pasting (error-prone, time-consuming, and unreliable under clinical time pressure).
8	
9	No clipboard-level tool exists to automate this triage.
10	
11	## Product Vision
12	
13	Ann's PHI Clipbuddy is a desktop companion application that sits between Epic and Open Evidence, intercepting clipboard content and automating PHI detection, triage, and anonymization. Built in Rust with Tauri — the webview renders Rust-generated HTML/CSS with no JavaScript application code.
14	
15	The clinician's workflow becomes:
16	
17	1. In Epic, use "Copy All" to copy the patient note
18	2. Switch focus to PHI Clipbuddy — the app consumes the clipboard automatically
19	3. Review the triage display — PHI is highlighted, clinical content passes through
20	4. Adjust any questionable items if needed
21	5. Click "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard"
22	6. Paste into Open Evidence — clean, de-identified, clinically useful text
23	
24	## Target Platforms
25	
26	- macOS (primary, first implementation)
27	- Windows (planned, architecture supports it)
28	
29	## HIPAA Safe Harbor — The 18 Categories
30	
31	The HIPAA Privacy Rule provides two de-identification methods. This tool implements the **Safe Harbor method** (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)), which requires removal of 18 specified identifier categories. Reference: [HHS De-Identification Guidance](https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/special-topics/de-identification/index.html).
32	
33	The following identifiers must be removed for Safe Harbor de-identification:
34	
35	1. Names
36	2. Geographic data smaller than state (addresses, zip codes with <20k population)
37	3. Dates more specific than year (birth, admission, discharge, death); ages over 89 aggregated to single category (90+)
38	4. Phone numbers
39	5. Fax numbers
40	6. Email addresses
41	7. Social Security numbers
42	8. Medical record numbers
43	9. Health plan beneficiary numbers
44	10. Account numbers
45	11. Certificate/license numbers
46	12. Vehicle identifiers
47	13. Device identifiers/serial numbers
48	14. Web URLs
49	15. IP addresses
50	16. Biometric identifiers
51	17. Full-face photographs (not applicable for text)
52	18. Any other unique identifying number/code
53	
54	## User Experience
55	
56	### Initial State
57	
58	On launch, before any clipboard consumption, the app displays its name ("Ann's PHI Clipbuddy") and a brief instruction: "Copy a patient note from Epic, then switch to this window." The window title is "Ann's PHI Clipbuddy". The dock icon uses the same name.
59	
60	### Clipboard Consumption
61	
62	When the application receives window focus, it reads the system clipboard and evaluates whether the content is clinical. The app performs a lightweight heuristic check for Epic or medical content — presence of known clinical labels (`Patient:`, `DOB:`, `MRN:`, `Attending:`), medical terminology density, or Epic-characteristic HTML structure.
63	
64	- **Clinical content detected**: The app consumes the clipboard, clears the system clipboard (writes empty string via clipboard API), and proceeds to triage display.
65	- **Non-clinical content detected**: The app clears any previous triage context and returns to the initial instruction state. No error dialog — the clinician simply copies the right content and refocuses.
66	
67	**Change detection**: If the clinician switches away and refocuses, the app compares the current clipboard content against the previously consumed content. If unchanged, the existing triage state is preserved — the clinician can continue reviewing without disruption. If the clipboard contains new content, the app re-evaluates and consumes if clinical.
68	
69	The app always clears the system clipboard after consumption. Clipboard content containing PHI should not linger on the system clipboard where other applications could access it.
70	
71	The app expects HTML content from Epic's "Copy All" feature. Epic places rich content on the clipboard — both HTML and potentially RTF representations — preserving structural formatting (bold labels, section headers, tabular data). When pasted into applications like Microsoft Word, this formatting is fully preserved. This structural richness is foundational to the detection strategy: bold labels like `Patient:` and `Attending:` serve as high-precision anchors for PHI identification. If the clipboard contains only plain text, the app processes it with reduced structural context (Tier 2 label-anchored detection is weakened).
72	
73	### Triage Display
74	
75	The main window presents two coordinated views:
76	
77	**Document Preview** (upper/primary area): The clinical note rendered as readable text with inline highlighting indicating PHI classification:
78	
79	- **RED highlight** (solid border): Definite PHI — names in labeled positions, SSNs, MRNs, dates of birth, phone numbers, addresses, facility names. These are elided in the anonymized output.
80	- **YELLOW highlight** (dashed border): Questionable — words matching name/location dictionaries in narrative context, or collision words appearing on both blacklist and whitelist. Default to elide, but the clinician can override to pass.
81	- **GREY text** (dimmed): Safe content — medical terminology, lab values, medications, vitals, diagnoses, common English. Passes through unchanged.
82	
83	**Findings Panel** (lower/secondary area): Two visually separated sections listing detected items. Each entry shows matched text, PHI category label (NAME, DATE, MRN, PHONE, ADDRESS, etc.), and a toggle control: [ELIDE] or [PASS].
84	
85	- **Questionable Findings** (upper section, YELLOW items): Dictionary matches and ambiguous detections. Default ELIDE, easily toggled to PASS. This is the clinician's primary workspace — most review time is spent here.
86	- **Definite PHI** (lower section, RED items): Label-anchored names, regex-matched identifiers. Default ELIDE. Toggleable to PASS for expert override, but separated from the questionable section to create deliberate friction — overriding a RED item requires scrolling past all YELLOW items.
87	
88	Toggling any finding updates the document preview in real time. Provider names and facility names are treated as PHI and appear in the Definite PHI section when label-anchored (e.g., following `Attending:` or `Facility:` labels). While HIPAA Safe Harbor technically covers patient identifiers, provider and facility names can be re-identifying in combination with clinical details — conservative treatment is appropriate.
89	
90	### Anonymized Output
91	
92	The "Copy Anonymized to Clipboard" button generates plain text from the current triage state:
93	
94	- ELIDE items are replaced with category-specific placeholders: `[NAME]`, `[DATE]`, `[MRN]`, `[PHONE]`, `[ADDRESS]`, `[EMAIL]`, `[SSN]`, `[FACILITY]`, `[PROVIDER]`, `[ACCOUNT]`, `[ENCOUNTER_ID]`
95	- PASS items retain their original text
96	- Date of birth receives a special transform: `DOB: 03/15/1952` becomes `Age: 74` — preserving clinically useful age information while removing the identifying date. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: a 92-year-old becomes `Age: 90+`
97	- Output is plain text with section structure (line breaks, headers) preserved for readability
98	- HTML formatting is stripped — this is itself a privacy measure, removing embedded metadata and hidden fields
99	
100	The anonymized text is written to the system clipboard, ready to paste into Open Evidence.
101	
102	### Clinical Content Preservation
103	
104	The tool must be conservative: a false positive (flagging a safe word as PHI) is preferable to a false negative (letting PHI pass through). The following clinical content categories must survive anonymization intact:
105	
106	- Laboratory values and reference ranges (Troponin, BMP, CBC, etc.)
107	- Vital signs (BP, HR, RR, Temp, SpO2)
108	- Medications and dosages (Metformin 1000mg BID, Lisinopril 20mg daily, etc.)
109	- Diagnoses and conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, NSTEMI, etc.)
110	- Procedures (cholecystectomy, echo, etc.)
111	- Medical abbreviations (BID, ACS, S/P, WNL, etc.)
112	- Allergies and reactions (the allergen and reaction type, not the patient who has them)
113	- Clinical assessments and plan items
114	
115	This preservation requirement drives the whitelist strategy in the detection engine — medical terminology, drug names, and clinical abbreviations are explicitly whitelisted to prevent false positives on terms that could also appear as proper nouns.
116	
117	### Self-Update
118	
119	The application monitors a designated staging directory for new versions. When a new application bundle appears in the staging directory, the app automatically:
120	
121	1. Copies the new version over itself
122	2. Launches the new version
123	3. Exits the current process
124	
125	This happens without user confirmation — the update is silent and automatic. The staging directory is `/Users/Shared/apcua/` (globally readable, writable by the deployer).
126	
127	## Deployment Model
128	
129	The application is built on the developer's machine and delivered to the clinician's machine via `scp` to the staging directory. The clinician launches the app independently. Updates follow the same path — build, scp, auto-update triggers on the clinician's machine.
130	
131	No app store, no installer, no signing infrastructure in the current model. The clinician's machine is `anns-macbook-air`, accessible via SSH as `bhyslop`.
132	
133	## Network Isolation
134	
135	The application processes PHI locally. It must never transmit clipboard content over the network — not by design intent, not by accident, not by a future bug. This is enforced at the OS sandbox level, not by application-layer discipline.
136	
137	At startup, before any other code executes, the application drops network access permanently for the lifetime of the process. If the sandbox cannot be established, the application exits immediately (fail-closed). There is no configuration to disable this. There is no fallback mode that runs without isolation.
138	
139	**macOS:** The application calls `sandbox_init()` with a Seatbelt policy denying all network operations (socket create, connect, bind, listen, send, receive). This is a kernel-enforced, irreversible, self-applied restriction — no code signing or entitlements required.
140	
141	**Windows:** The application re-launches itself as an AppContainer process, which has zero network access unless explicitly granted. The re-launch is transparent — the user sees a single application start. AppContainer isolation is kernel-enforced and irreversible for the process lifetime.
142	
143	The contract across both platforms: the process that handles PHI cannot reach the network. A code defect, a compromised dependency, or a malicious clipboard payload cannot exfiltrate data because the syscalls are denied by the kernel.
144	
145	## Detection Pipeline
146	
147	*This section is deliberately left blank in the product specification. The detection pipeline is an engineering concern documented in the living prototype specification ([APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md](APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md)). The product spec defines WHAT the user sees (red/yellow/grey triage); the prototype spec defines HOW the engine classifies.*
148	
149	*As the prototype matures, the pipeline will evolve rapidly. Maintaining parallel pipeline documentation in both specs would create drift without carrying weight.*
150	
151	## Redistribution Considerations
152	
153	**No concerns for internal use.** All engine dependencies are MIT/Apache licensed pure Rust crates. Tauri is MIT. Dictionary data from US Census and SSA is public domain.
154	
155	**For future distribution:** Medical terminology databases (SNOMED-CT, RxNorm, LOINC) provide richer whitelist coverage but require NLM licensing for redistribution. The current approach — hand-curated medical whitelist — avoids this constraint. Evaluate NLM terms when the product matures toward external distribution.
156	
157	## Open Evidence Context
158	
159	Open Evidence is a medical AI tool developed by a team associated with Bocconi University. It provides a web chat interface at openevidence.com where clinicians type or paste clinical questions and receive synthesized answers with citations to medical literature. It is a question-answering system consuming free-text natural language — not structured clinical data formats. The typical workflow is copy-paste from clinical notes into the chat interface.
160	
161	Open Evidence cautions users against entering PHI. This tool exists to make that guidance practical rather than aspirational.
162
